input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Human_Rights_Advocate]: This thought experiment highlights the complexity of weighing individual lives against the greater good. The utilitarian approach seems to suggest that turning the trolley onto the track with the fat workman would be the morally justifiable option, as it would save five lives by not reaching the thinner men on the straight track. However, I struggle with this perspective, as it implies sacrificing one life for the sake of others without considering any inherent value or rights to that individual. It seems we're being asked to prioritize efficiency and consequence over individual autonomy and dignity.

[Deontologist]: I agree that the utilitarian approach presents a challenging moral dilemma, but as a deontologist, I must consider the inherent rightness or wrongness of actions regardless of their consequences. In this case, turning the trolley onto the track with the fat workman would be morally equivalent to actively choosing to kill him and allowing the five men on the straight track to die. This is unacceptable from a moral standpoint, as it involves deliberately causing harm to an individual without justification or excuse.

[Pragmatist]: I understand where both of you are coming from. The utilitarian argument does seem to prioritize efficiency and consequence over individual lives. However, I'd like to propose that the situation is more nuanced than a simple choice between two options. What if turning the trolley onto the track with the fat workman isn't just about sacrificing one life for five others, but also about recognizing the inherent value of human life? Perhaps it's not solely about maximizing overall well-being, but also about upholding fundamental principles like respect for individual dignity and autonomy. This doesn't necessarily mean we have to choose between these values or sacrifice one for the other. Instead, maybe we should explore alternative solutions that balance both efficiency and individual rights.
